did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it implements and evaluates a proposed DCNN, VGG16, and VGG19 on the HAM10000 dataset to classify dermoscopic images into seven categories of skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors designed, implemented, trained, and evaluated a proposed Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) alongside VGG16 and VGG19 models on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections detailing dataset pre-processing, architecture design, and training parameters, followed by a results section comparing performance metrics (accuracy and loss) of the new models against existing ones.

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) for Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer classification by implementing and evaluating a proposed Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) alongside VGG16 and VGG19 models on the HAM10000 dataset. To handle class imbalance, the authors applied data augmentation techniques to balance the categories before training. The proposed DCNN was trained from scratch, while VGG16 and VGG19 were fine-tuned using transfer learning. Performance was evaluated based on overall average accuracy and loss metrics over 100 epochs. The proposed DCNN achieved a testing accuracy of 99% and a testing loss of 0.02, outperforming VGG16 (96% accuracy) and VGG19 (94% accuracy). The results indicate that the proposed DCNN is superior in classification performance and does not suffer from overfitting.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six cancer types in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Had a significantly higher number of samples before augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Seven categories of skin lesions (Melanocytic Nevi, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Vascular Lesions, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements and evaluates Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN, VGG16, VGG19) to classify dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The task is explicitly described as 'skin cancer classification' and 'classifying skin cancer types'. The model uses a softmax output layer for seven different categories. Performance is measured by accuracy and loss, not continuous regression metrics.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine 10000)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing 10,015 images with corresponding metadata (patient age, sex, location). The dataset was highly imbalanced initially, with a severe lack of samples in VASC and DF categories and a high number in NV. Data augmentation was applied to balance the classes, resulting in a final dataset size of 7,182 images with approximately 1,000 samples per category.

per_class.NV: Significantly higher than others (exact count not reported, but reduced by ~5000 during balancing)
per_class.DF: Severe lack of samples (exact count not reported)
per_class.VASC: Severe lack of samples (exact count not reported)

use.training: 80% of the augmented dataset was used for training.
use.validation: 20% of the training data (i.e., 16% of the total augmented dataset) was used for validation during training.
use.testing: The remaining 20% of the dataset was used for testing.
use.other: Metadata was not utilized in the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed DCNN, VGG16, and VGG19 were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy and loss) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed DCNN showed superiority in terms of both overall average accuracy and loss compared to VGG16 and VGG19. The small difference between training and testing accuracy and loss for the proposed DCNN indicates that the network did not overfit. VGG16 accuracy increased steadily until reaching an asymptotic of 100% during training, while its loss decayed rapidly below 0.2. The confusion matrix for the proposed DCNN illustrated that the vast majority of predicted results fell under the correct categories.

architecture_used.main_model: Proposed Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN), VGG16, VGG19
architecture_used.pretraining: Proposed DCNN: Trained from scratch. VGG16: Fine-tuned using Transfer Learning (TL). VGG19: Fine-tuned using Transfer Learning (TL).

proposed_dcnn.structure: 3 repetitions of (Convolutional Layer + Max Pooling Layer), followed by a Flattening Layer, then 2 Densely Connected Layers.

vgg16.structure: 2 repetitions of (2 Convolutional Layers + Pooling Layer), followed by 3 repetitions of (3 Convolutional Layers + Pooling Layer), followed by 3 Densely Connected Layers.

vgg19.structure: Enhanced version of VGG16 with more depth.

training_details.data_split: 80% training, 20% testing. Of the training data, 20% was used for validation.
training_details.best_model_selection: Parameters extracted from the epoch providing the best training accuracy and loss.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to handle class imbalance, specifically applied to categories BCC, AKIEC, VASC, and DF.
Augmentation methods included: crop, scale, contrast adjustment, brightness adjustment, horizontal flip, vertical flip, and combinations of these.
Removal of approximately 5000 samples from the NV category to balance the dataset.
Final balanced dataset size of 7182 images with approximately 1000 samples per category.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal flip, vertical flip, crop, and scale were applied to the BCC, AKIEC, VASC, and DF categories.
Contrast adjustment and brightness adjustment were applied to the BCC, AKIEC, VASC, and DF categories.

Approximately 5000 samples were removed from the NV (Melanocytic Nevi) category to balance the dataset, resulting in approximately 1000 samples per category for the final augmented dataset of 7182 images.

conclusions: The proposed Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) achieved a testing accuracy of 99% and a testing loss of 0.02 on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming VGG16 (96% accuracy) and VGG19 (94% accuracy).
The proposed DCNN demonstrated superiority in terms of both overall average accuracy and loss compared to the fine-tuned VGG16 and VGG19 models.
The small difference between training and testing accuracy and loss for the proposed DCNN indicates that the network did not suffer from overfitting.
Data augmentation was necessary to handle the severe class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, particularly for categories with severe sample lacks like VASC and DF.

The metadata provided in the HAM10000 dataset (patient age, sex, location) was not utilized in the current study, and exploiting this information is identified as a future direction to potentially boost performance and robustness.
Future work includes testing the networks on other skin cancer datasets, such as those from the ISIC challenge, to make the network resilient to all types of dermoscopic images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000) without external validation, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset was artificially balanced by removing approximately 5000 samples from the Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class and augmenting underrepresented classes (VASC, DF, BCC, AKIEC), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could introduce bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported performance differences between the proposed DCNN and VGG16/VGG19.
Patient metadata (age, sex, location) available in the HAM10000 dataset was not utilized, representing a missed opportunity to potentially improve model robustness and performance.

The proposed DCNN was trained from scratch while VGG16 and VGG19 were fine-tuned using transfer learning; this asymmetry in training methodology may confound the comparison of architectural efficiency versus pre-training benefits.
The paper does not report per-class accuracy or sensitivity/specificity metrics, only overall average accuracy and loss, making it difficult to assess performance on critical classes like Melanoma.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope; future work is acknowledged to include testing on other datasets (e.g., ISIC challenge) to improve resilience to diverse dermoscopic images.
